title: Cavity-mediated localization and collective electron correlation phases
authors: Dominik Sidler, Michael Ruggenthaler, Angel Rubio
abstract: Collective strong coupling of molecular ensembles to optical cavities opens a route to modifying matter through genuinely collective electronic correlations. Yet even in the absence of a cavity, Coulomb correlations are notoriously difficult to describe, and cavity coupling adds transverse correlation channels extending over the entire molecular ensemble. Here we show that this seemingly intractable problem admits a controlled description by mapping the collective intermolecular electronic correlations to the analytically solvable spherical Sherrington-Kirkpatrick model. The resulting theory predicts two collective correlation phases, a paracorrelated phase and a spin-glass correlation phase, beyond the conventional uncorrelated molecular regime. These phases reveal an entropy-driven localization-delocalization mechanism that transfers molecular electronic states into collectively correlated cavity-dressed states. Our work establishes cavity-mediated electron correlations as a microscopic mechanism for emergent phases in strongly coupled molecular ensembles.
summary: This paper shows that strong light-matter coupling in molecular cavities can induce new phases of electron correlation, such as spin-glass and paracorrelated phases. By mapping the system to the spherical Sherrington-Kirkpatrick model, the authors demonstrate that entropy can drive a localization-delocalization transition. This establishes a microscopic mechanism for emergent collective electronic behavior in cavity-dressed molecular ensembles.
main_problem: The paper addresses the difficulty of describing transverse, long-range electron correlations in molecular ensembles coupled to optical cavities, specifically investigating if these correlations can induce new collective phases.
main_result: The authors identify two new collective correlation phases—a paracorrelated phase and a spin-glass phase—driven by an entropy-mediated localization-delocalization mechanism.
method: The study employs an extension of the Configuration Interaction Singles (CIS) framework within a Reduced Density Matrix (RDM) theory, mapping the complex electronic correlations to the analytically solvable spherical Sherrington-Kirkpatrick (SSK) model.
model_or_system: A large ensemble of molecules placed within an optical cavity, where the Hamiltonian includes longitudinal Coulomb interactions and transverse dipole self-energy, effectively behaving as a spin glass due to random molecular orientations and the Pauli principle.
key_observables: Correlation energy, free energy, and the fraction of promoted electrons (epsilon).
important_parameters: Light-matter coupling strength (lambda), fluctuation strength (sigma), temperature (T), and the degeneracy of excited orbitals (N_deg).
main_assumptions: Assumes a cavity-Born-Oppenheimer partitioning, a quasi-dilute gas approximation, degenerate excited orbital energies, and that the thermodynamic limit (N to infinity) applies.
figures_summary: Figure 1 presents a phase diagram showing the transitions between Uncorrelated, Paracorrelated, and Spin Glass phases as a function of temperature and fluctuation strength.
paper_structure: The paper begins by defining the Hamiltonian and the problem of transverse correlations, introduces a mapping to the SSK model, derives the free energy and phase transition conditions, and concludes with the identification of emergent entropic phases.
why_interesting: It provides a theoretical bridge between polaritonic chemistry and spin glass physics, demonstrating how cavity-mediated interactions can create emergent many-body phases through entropic stabilization.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Collective correlations in an ensemble of molecules subject to collective strong coupling in an optical cavity. The cavity-mediated synchronization of the electron fluctuations, give rise to random transversal electron-electron interactions (fluctuations) described by σ (see Eq. (9)). The ensemble HF energy to excite N- electrons to a collectively correlated state is given by ∆E ∝N. If the entropy of this highly degenerate Ndeg collective state dominates, transversal electron correla- tions emerge, and the para-correlated phase (paramagnetic in the spin glass model) is entered.
